title: Evening Wrap: ASX 200 slips as tech gets rekt — gold and copper stocks’ dream run continues as lithium’s rally stalls
content:
The S&P/ASX 200 closed 6.4 points lower, down 0.07%.

Selling pressure returned to the Information Technology (XIJ) (-1.9%) sector today, which made near two-year lows on the back of heavy falls in Xero (XRO) (-3.6%) and Wisetech Global (WTC) (-2.2%).

As it tends to be the case more often than not, balancing out the tech sector's misfortune, the Gold (XGD) (+1.3%) sector showed no signs of slowing its ascent as spot prices blasted through US$5,500 an ounce. Copper stocks did much the same, as the benchmark COMEX copper futures contract leaped to all time highs. This drove strong gains in the likes of Sandfire Resources (SFR) (+5.2%) and Capstone Copper Corp. (+4.5%).

In stocks, Woodside Energy (WDS) (+0.6%) posted a solid December quarter result led by Sangomar and Mad Dog, but its 2026 production outlook fell short of consensus. Market operator ASX (ASX) (+7.5%) jumped after yesterday's market update hit the spot with JP Morgan, with the investment bank upgrading their rating on the stock to Overweight from Neutral.

Elsewhere, a pullback in the price of the benchmark lithium contract in China triggered an (arguably!) outsized correction in local lithium stocks. IGO (IGO) (-5.8%) and Pilbara Minerals (PLS) (-4.6%) bore the brunt of the selling.

Be sure to click/scroll through for the usual reporting of the major sector and stock-specific moves, the broker responses to them, as well as all the key economic data in tonight's Evening Wrap.

Also, I have detailed technical analysis on the Nasdaq Composite and the S&P/ASX 200 in today's ChartWatch.

Let's dive in!

Markets
ASX 200 Session Chart

The S&P/ASX 200 (XJO) finished 6.4 points lower at 8,927.5, 0.07% from its session high and 0.77% from its low. In the broader-based S&P/ASX 300 (XKO) advancers trailed decliners by 104 to 171.

Fund flows: The trend is your friend until... 📈📉

Sometimes markets will make you feel like a smarty pants. It comes with the territory as a trend follower because you’re not fighting the market all the time, you’re going with it.

When global funds are flowing in a particular direction, they can do so for many months as the major macroeconomic themes that drive them can play out over substantially long timeframes. This pervasive flow of money — particularly when sloshing past a tiny-Tin-Pot-market like ours — tends to grow the trends you’ve attached yourself to each day.

But never confuse being on the right side of a trend with intelligence or excellent judgment! What the trend gives, it's bound to take away when it eventually bends… and ends!

Take your wins — but stay level headed — and always remain attentive for signals in the trends, price action, candles, and volume that the balance between demand and supply is shifting. ⚖️

What’s my point? 🤔

Yes, yes, I was getting there… Stop interrupting! 😉

Today was yet another day where fund flows went almost exactly as we’d expect based on the prevailing trends:

Winner Sectors (Short duration / Pricing Power)

Gold Sub-Index (XGD) (+1.3%)

Resources (XJR) (+1.0%)

Energy (XEJ) (+0.9%)

Loser Sectors (Long duration / Consumer Discretionary / Bond Proxies)

Information Technology (XIJ) (-1.9%)

Consumer Discretionary (XDJ) (-0.8%)

Health Care (XHJ) (-0.7%)

Communication Services (XTJ) (-0.7%)

Swinger Sector (Bond / Proxies)

Financials (XFJ) (-0.3%)

And, yes, there’s a MOTN (More Often Than Not) aspect associated with those prevailing trends which suggests they’ll likely do the same tomorrow. But the future is unknown! So, never assume! ⚠️

But do: Follow the Trend + Manage Risk = Analyse, Accept, and Act! 💪💪💪

Stock specific: How tech got rekt (and how it may rise from the ashes! 🐦‍🔥)

Today saw the lowest close for the Tech sector nearly 2-years — and that’s even accounting for dividends (i.e., adding them back to the “cash” chart shown below).

It’s been a slow train wreck for tech, which started this downturn with a sector P/E ratio close to 100. The present short and long term downtrends are well-set, the price action is synonymous with sell the rally and panic-selling at the lows, and the candles suggest pervasive hedge fund administered algorithmic sell orders. ❌❌❌

Information Technology Sector Index (XIJ) chart

I know there’s only one thing I want to do when I look at a chart like the one above. -R (i.e., reduce my risk exposure, or even better — go short). Certainly my ChartWatch ASX Scans downtrends watchlist has been awash with the biggest names from the XIJ for the last 4-months.

But, as they say in the classics, it’s always darkest before the dawn. As a trend follower, it's impossible for me to pick the low of this trend. It could be today for all I know. 🤷

However, there's one thing that I am sure of: When demand grows more confident and supply less desperate… the balance will swing from the present excess supply to equilibrium, and then after time, back to excess demand. It’s very unlikely that this transition occurs overnight — hence the other classic market saying: Beware catching the falling sword!

If you’re oblivious to the mechanics of such change, then you’re destined never to see it. When it does come, it will feel like a surprise: “Oh, when did that happen… Darn! I knew I should have bought the dip in tech!”.

If you’re attentive, as I trust you are now, then you’ll look at the above chart with a sense of awe: How incredible fund flows are! What a massive impact they can have on our market! And most importantly, what tremendous opportunities they create for us trend followers: both on the way down (shorting), and on the way back up!

ChartWatch
Nasdaq Composite Index
Analysis

The regular financial press headlines will claim a win for the Comp on Wednesday, but we know that the black-bodied candle suggests otherwise: there was some modest excess supply in the system in the shadow of the 24020 all-time high.

But, looking that the size of that black-body, and considering the diminutive stature of Wednesday's candle overall, plus the smidge of downward pointing shadow (i.e., a hint of demand-side participation) — it was definitely not a dominant supply-side showing.

Rather, it remains perfectly consistent with the prevailing set of demand-side controlled technicals:

Short term / Long term trends: ⬆️/⬆️ (agreement between short and long term traders / investors) ✅

Price Action: Rising peaks and rising troughs (i.e. consistent with supply removal and demand reinforcement, buy the dip + FOMO into rallies). ✅

Candles: Predominantly demand-side in nature (i.e. consistent with pervasive programmed buy orders, lack of strong opposing sell orders). ✅

Volume: Benign, nothing special going on there… which is okay… symptomatic of very little motivated / large supply. ✅

✅+✅+✅+✅+✅ = Stay the course = No change to present risk settings.

I accept that many who are reading my work for the first time this week may consider it to be utterly boring: “This guy says the same bloody thing all the time… demand… blah… supply… blah… trend is up… stay the course… blah-blah-blahdy-blah-blah…” 🥱💤

If that’s you, then you’d be correct! I’ve been called a lot of things by people in this industry over the last 3-decades, but I hope you’ll agree on this description: Consistent.

Of course, I could predict the exact day/time and precise price the Nasdaq Composite will peak and then crash… but that would be a load of 🐂. Because I can’t. Because that’s impossible to do with any consistency. Because the future is unknown = Prediction is futile!

There’s an old saying among computer programmers: Garbage in equals garbage out. It means poor inputs produce poor results. 🤖

Trading works the same way. If your rules, data, or decision process are sloppy, inconsistent, or emotional, the outcomes will be unreliable — no matter how good you think your strategy is.

Consistency is the most important habit a trader can develop. By applying the same rules the same way, every time, you can clearly see what’s working and what isn’t.

Losses become information, not confusion, allowing you to refine entries, exits, and risk management with purpose. Basically, this means: stop doing the stuff that consistently causes you losses. 🛑

Wins, equally, are information, confirming which parts of your edge should be repeated and reinforced. Basically, this means: do more of the stuff that consistently causes you to win! 🏆

The opposite of consistency is randomness. ⚠️⚠️⚠️

A random trader cannot diagnose mistakes because there is no stable process to analyse. Random in equals random out! This is why being consistent and wrong is still better than being random and right —because only consistency creates learning, improvement, and long-term success.

I bet you know ten investors you’d describe as “random and right” — achieving occasional success, but whose lack of consistency ensures they never truly progress in the long run. I trust you’re not one of the ten, and instead are on your way to becoming a more consistent trader. 😉

View

I remain at a 2/3RP portfolio risk limit 🪣 (RP = Risk Position — it reflects my personal allowable capital allocation limit for my investments in US stocks. So 1/2RP is 50%, 2/3RP is 67% and FRP is 100%). A credible demand-side candle from here will trigger a move to FRP vs a close below 22692 would trigger a move to 1/2RP.

Key levels

The next zone of demand is 22692-916. If the Comp closes below that zone, we can assume the supply side is in control. On a close below 21898 the long term uptrend is likely under significant pressure = ⚠️. 23722-24020 is the nearest critical zone of supply — the Comp must close within/above this zone with a strong demand-side candle to confirm the demand-side is unequivocally back in control of the Comp's price.

S&P/ASX 200 (XJO)
Analysis

Hey, we don’t predict — but is it fair to say that today’s candle is almost exactly what we’d expect under present circumstances? As in, a modest follow-through of yesterday’s indecision early, but finishing consistent with the broader environment of demand-side control. 🤔

The day started with supply-side control — likely as international fund managers reacted to yesterday’s inflation data… But soon, the demand-side took over, buying the dip, and then creating an environment of excess demand all the way into the close.

The result is the tell-tale downward pointing shadow that we associate with buy the dip activity. The papers will call today a “down-day”, but we know it was hardly that.

Hey, it wasn’t an blowout demand-side triumph either — we still closed with a small black-body — but it’s an important piece of the puzzle that helps us ascertain how much motivation the supply-side presently has (probably not much), and how much confidence the demand-side has retained in the face of some bad news (probably quite a bit).

Most importantly, today’s candle guides our risk management: Stay the course.

View

I remain comfortable at 2/3RP 🪣 (i.e., my personal allowable capital allocation limit for my investments in Australian stocks is now 67%).

Key levels

The short term trend ribbon is the closest zone of (dynamic) demand (presently 8785-8820). 8765 is the closest static point of demand. A close below it/the short term uptrend ribbon would neutralise the present short term uptrend, while a close below 8675 almost certainly would end it. The October all-time highs around 9110-9116 is the next key zone of supply.

summary:
• The ASX 200 closed slightly lower, down 0.07%, as selling pressure hit the technology sector.
• The Information Technology sector experienced significant declines, with Xero (XRO) and Wisetech Global (WTC) leading the fall.
• Gold and copper stocks continued their strong performance, driven by rising commodity prices.
• Lithium stocks saw a correction due to a pullback in Chinese lithium contract prices, with IGO (IGO) and Pilbara Minerals (PLS) being notably affected.
• Woodside Energy (WDS) reported a solid December quarter, while market operator ASX (ASX) gained on a JPMorgan upgrade.

🎯 My Portfolio:
• APX - Was listed as a top gainer, with a significant increase in price.
• NVX - Not mentioned in the article.
• ZIP - Not mentioned in the article.
• DRO - Deterra Royalties (DRR) was downgraded to hold from buy at Ord Minnett.

📈 Stocks to Watch (Bullish):
• ASX (ASX) - The market operator gained 7.5% after a positive rating upgrade from JP Morgan.
• Sandfire Resources (SFR) - This copper stock rose 5.2% on the back of strong copper futures prices.
• Northern Star Resources (NST) - A gold miner that gained 3.1%, reflecting the strength in the gold sector.

📉 Stocks to Watch (Bearish):
• IGO (IGO) - This lithium stock fell 5.8% due to a correction in lithium prices.
• PLS Group (PLS) - Also affected by the lithium market downturn, PLS Group saw a 4.6% decline.
• Xero (XRO) - The tech stock dropped 3.6%, contributing to the broader sector's weakness.
formatted_content:
The S&P/ASX 200 closed 6.4 points lower, down 0.07%.

Selling pressure returned to the Information Technology (XIJ) (-1.9%) sector today, which made near two-year lows on the back of heavy falls in Xero (XRO) (-3.6%) and Wisetech Global (WTC) (-2.2%).

As it tends to be the case more often than not, balancing out the tech sector's misfortune, the Gold (XGD) (+1.3%) sector showed no signs of slowing its ascent as spot prices blasted through US$5,500 an ounce. Copper stocks did much the same, as the benchmark COMEX copper futures contract leaped to all time highs. This drove strong gains in the likes of Sandfire Resources (SFR) (+5.2%) and Capstone Copper Corp. (+4.5%).

In stocks, Woodside Energy (WDS) (+0.6%) posted a solid December quarter result led by Sangomar and Mad Dog, but its 2026 production outlook fell short of consensus. Market operator ASX (ASX) (+7.5%) jumped after yesterday's market update hit the spot with JP Morgan, with the investment bank upgrading their rating on the stock to Overweight from Neutral.

Elsewhere, a pullback in the price of the benchmark lithium contract in China triggered an (arguably!) outsized correction in local lithium stocks. IGO (IGO) (-5.8%) and Pilbara Minerals (PLS) (-4.6%) bore the brunt of the selling.

Be sure to click/scroll through for the usual reporting of the major sector and stock-specific moves, the broker responses to them, as well as all the key economic data in tonight's Evening Wrap.

Also, I have detailed technical analysis on the Nasdaq Composite and the S&P/ASX 200 in today's ChartWatch.

Let's dive in!

Markets

ASX 200 Session Chart

The S&P/ASX 200 (XJO) finished 6.4 points lower at 8,927.5, 0.07% from its session high and 0.77% from its low. In the broader-based S&P/ASX 300 (XKO) advancers trailed decliners by 104 to 171.

Fund flows: The trend is your friend until... 📈📉

Sometimes markets will make you feel like a smarty pants. It comes with the territory as a trend follower because you’re not fighting the market all the time, you’re going with it.

When global funds are flowing in a particular direction, they can do so for many months as the major macroeconomic themes that drive them can play out over substantially long timeframes. This pervasive flow of money — particularly when sloshing past a tiny-Tin-Pot-market like ours — tends to grow the trends you’ve attached yourself to each day.

But never confuse being on the right side of a trend with intelligence or excellent judgment! What the trend gives, it's bound to take away when it eventually bends… and ends!

Take your wins — but stay level headed — and always remain attentive for signals in the trends, price action, candles, and volume that the balance between demand and supply is shifting. ⚖️

What’s my point? 🤔

Yes, yes, I was getting there… Stop interrupting! 😉

Today was yet another day where fund flows went almost exactly as we’d expect based on the prevailing trends:

Winner Sectors (Short duration / Pricing Power)

  • Gold Sub-Index (XGD) (+1.3%)
  • Resources (XJR) (+1.0%)
  • Energy (XEJ) (+0.9%)

Loser Sectors (Long duration / Consumer Discretionary / Bond Proxies)

  • Information Technology (XIJ) (-1.9%)
  • Consumer Discretionary (XDJ) (-0.8%)
  • Health Care (XHJ) (-0.7%)
  • Communication Services (XTJ) (-0.7%)

Swinger Sector (Bond / Proxies)

  • Financials (XFJ) (-0.3%)

And, yes, there’s a MOTN (More Often Than Not) aspect associated with those prevailing trends which suggests they’ll likely do the same tomorrow. But the future is unknown! So, never assume! ⚠️

But do: Follow the Trend + Manage Risk = Analyse, Accept, and Act! 💪💪💪

Stock specific: How tech got rekt (and how it may rise from the ashes! 🐦‍🔥)

Today saw the lowest close for the Tech sector nearly 2-years — and that’s even accounting for dividends (i.e., adding them back to the “cash” chart shown below).

It’s been a slow train wreck for tech, which started this downturn with a sector P/E ratio close to 100. The present short and long term downtrends are well-set, the price action is synonymous with sell the rally and panic-selling at the lows, and the candles suggest pervasive hedge fund administered algorithmic sell orders. ❌❌❌

Information Technology Sector Index (XIJ) chart

I know there’s only one thing I want to do when I look at a chart like the one above. -R (i.e., reduce my risk exposure, or even better — go short). Certainly my ChartWatch ASX Scans downtrends watchlist has been awash with the biggest names from the XIJ for the last 4-months.

But, as they say in the classics, it’s always darkest before the dawn. As a trend follower, it's impossible for me to pick the low of this trend. It could be today for all I know. 🤷

However, there's one thing that I am sure of: When demand grows more confident and supply less desperate… the balance will swing from the present excess supply to equilibrium, and then after time, back to excess demand. It’s very unlikely that this transition occurs overnight — hence the other classic market saying: Beware catching the falling sword!

If you’re oblivious to the mechanics of such change, then you’re destined never to see it. When it does come, it will feel like a surprise: “Oh, when did that happen… Darn! I knew I should have bought the dip in tech!”.

If you’re attentive, as I trust you are now, then you’ll look at the above chart with a sense of awe: How incredible fund flows are! What a massive impact they can have on our market! And most importantly, what tremendous opportunities they create for us trend followers: both on the way down (shorting), and on the way back up!

ChartWatch

Nasdaq Composite Index

Analysis

The regular financial press headlines will claim a win for the Comp on Wednesday, but we know that the black-bodied candle suggests otherwise: there was some modest excess supply in the system in the shadow of the 24020 all-time high.

But, looking that the size of that black-body, and considering the diminutive stature of Wednesday's candle overall, plus the smidge of downward pointing shadow (i.e., a hint of demand-side participation) — it was definitely not a dominant supply-side showing.

Rather, it remains perfectly consistent with the prevailing set of demand-side controlled technicals:

  • Short term / Long term trends: ⬆️/⬆️ (agreement between short and long term traders / investors) ✅
  • Price Action: Rising peaks and rising troughs (i.e. consistent with supply removal and demand reinforcement, buy the dip + FOMO into rallies). ✅
  • Candles: Predominantly demand-side in nature (i.e. consistent with pervasive programmed buy orders, lack of strong opposing sell orders). ✅
  • Volume: Benign, nothing special going on there… which is okay… symptomatic of very little motivated / large supply. ✅

✅+✅+✅+✅+✅ = Stay the course = No change to present risk settings.

I accept that many who are reading my work for the first time this week may consider it to be utterly boring: “This guy says the same bloody thing all the time… demand… blah… supply… blah… trend is up… stay the course… blah-blah-blahdy-blah-blah…” 🥱💤

If that’s you, then you’d be correct! I’ve been called a lot of things by people in this industry over the last 3-decades, but I hope you’ll agree on this description: Consistent.

Of course, I could predict the exact day/time and precise price the Nasdaq Composite will peak and then crash… but that would be a load of 🐂. Because I can’t. Because that’s impossible to do with any consistency. Because the future is unknown = Prediction is futile!

There’s an old saying among computer programmers: Garbage in equals garbage out. It means poor inputs produce poor results. 🤖

Trading works the same way. If your rules, data, or decision process are sloppy, inconsistent, or emotional, the outcomes will be unreliable — no matter how good you think your strategy is.

Consistency is the most important habit a trader can develop. By applying the same rules the same way, every time, you can clearly see what’s working and what isn’t.

Losses become information, not confusion, allowing you to refine entries, exits, and risk management with purpose. Basically, this means: stop doing the stuff that consistently causes you losses. 🛑

Wins, equally, are information, confirming which parts of your edge should be repeated and reinforced. Basically, this means: do more of the stuff that consistently causes you to win! 🏆

The opposite of consistency is randomness. ⚠️⚠️⚠️

A random trader cannot diagnose mistakes because there is no stable process to analyse. Random in equals random out! This is why being consistent and wrong is still better than being random and right —because only consistency creates learning, improvement, and long-term success.

I bet you know ten investors you’d describe as “random and right” — achieving occasional success, but whose lack of consistency ensures they never truly progress in the long run. I trust you’re not one of the ten, and instead are on your way to becoming a more consistent trader. 😉

View

I remain at a 2/3RP portfolio risk limit 🪣 (RP = Risk Position — it reflects my personal allowable capital allocation limit for my investments in US stocks. So 1/2RP is 50%, 2/3RP is 67% and FRP is 100%). A credible demand-side candle from here will trigger a move to FRP vs a close below 22692 would trigger a move to 1/2RP.

Key levels

The next zone of demand is 22692-916. If the Comp closes below that zone, we can assume the supply side is in control. On a close below 21898 the long term uptrend is likely under significant pressure = ⚠️. 23722-24020 is the nearest critical zone of supply — the Comp must close within/above this zone with a strong demand-side candle to confirm the demand-side is unequivocally back in control of the Comp's price.

S&P/ASX 200 (XJO)

Analysis

Hey, we don’t predict — but is it fair to say that today’s candle is almost exactly what we’d expect under present circumstances? As in, a modest follow-through of yesterday’s indecision early, but finishing consistent with the broader environment of demand-side control. 🤔

The day started with supply-side control — likely as international fund managers reacted to yesterday’s inflation data… But soon, the demand-side took over, buying the dip, and then creating an environment of excess demand all the way into the close.

The result is the tell-tale downward pointing shadow that we associate with buy the dip activity. The papers will call today a “down-day”, but we know it was hardly that.

Hey, it wasn’t an blowout demand-side triumph either — we still closed with a small black-body — but it’s an important piece of the puzzle that helps us ascertain how much motivation the supply-side presently has (probably not much), and how much confidence the demand-side has retained in the face of some bad news (probably quite a bit).

Most importantly, today’s candle guides our risk management: Stay the course.

View

I remain comfortable at 2/3RP 🪣 (i.e., my personal allowable capital allocation limit for my investments in Australian stocks is now 67%).

Key levels

The short term trend ribbon is the closest zone of (dynamic) demand (presently 8785-8820). 8765 is the closest static point of demand. A close below it/the short term uptrend ribbon would neutralise the present short term uptrend, while a close below 8675 almost certainly would end it. The October all-time highs around 9110-9116 is the next key zone of supply.
